body.label: def:cocountable_topology
body.name: co-countable
body.definition: The co-countable topology on a set X is defined such that a subset U of X is open if and only if U = ∅ or X \ U is countable.

body.label: thm:cocountable_sequences

hypothesis.assumption: Let X be an uncountable set equipped with the co-countable topology.
Let (x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} be a sequence in X and let L \in X.

body.claim: A sequence (x_n) in X converges to a point L \in X if and only if the sequence is eventually constant with value L, i.e., there exists an integer N such that for all n \ge N, x_n = L.

proof.text: We prove that a sequence (x_n) in an uncountable set X with the co-countable topology converges to L if and only if it is eventually constant with value L.

proof.label: sec:forward_implication
proof.header: If the sequence converges, then it is eventually constant

content.assumption: Assume that the sequence (x_n) converges to L in the co-countable topology.

content.statement: Let A := { x_n \mid n \in \mathbb{N},\ x_n \neq L } \subseteq X be the set of all values taken by the sequence which are different from L.
content.properties: subset of X consisting of all values of the sequence different from L

content.proof_method: Since the sequence (x_n) is indexed by \mathbb{N}, the image set {x_n \mid n \in \mathbb{N}} is at most countable, and A is a subset of this image set.

content.claim: The set U is open in the co-countable topology and L \in U.
content.proof_method: Since A is countable, X \setminus A is open by the definition of the co-countable topology. Also, by construction A consists only of points different from L, so L \notin A and hence L \in U.

content.claim: There exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that for all n \ge N, x_n \in U.
content.proof_method: This follows from the definition of convergence of (x_n) to L, applied to the open set U containing L.

content.claim: For all n \ge N, we have x_n = L.
content.proof_method: For n \ge N, x_n \in U = X \setminus A, so x_n \notin A. By definition of A, the elements of A are exactly those x_n that are different from L, hence x_n \notin A implies x_n = L.

content.claim: If (x_n) converges to L, then (x_n) is eventually constant with value L.

proof.label: sec:reverse_implication
proof.header: If the sequence is eventually constant, then it converges

content.assumption: Assume that the sequence (x_n) is eventually constant with value L, i.e., there exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that for all n \ge N, x_n = L.
Let U \subseteq X be any open set such that L \in U.

content.claim: There exists M \in \mathbb{N} such that for all n \ge M, x_n \in U.
content.proof_method: By eventual constancy, for all n \ge N we have x_n = L. Since L \in U, it follows that for all n \ge N, x_n \in U, so we may take M = N.

content.claim: The sequence (x_n) converges to L in the co-countable topology.

proof.claim: Combining both directions, the sequence (x_n) converges to L if and only if it is eventually constant with value L.
